Data Collection 287
Data collection. All interactions and documents provided to participants were in French. Data were collected 288
by six individuals who were either first-language French users, or who had learned French as their 289
second/additional language at an advanced level. All procedures were approved by the Geneva Cantonal 290
Ethical Commission (CCER Protocol N. 2021-01004) prior to the beginning of the study. All participants 291
signed a waiver for anonymised data release in the public domain, and no identifying information was 292
retained, to the best of our knowledge. 293
 294
Data were collected in two online and two in-person sessions, hereafter named Session 1, 2, 3 and 295
4, which always occurred on different days but in the same order (1-4) between July 2022 and June 2023. 296
Session 1 was unsupervised, and required participants to fill out online questionnaires. Session 2 involved 297
online behavioural data collection, and was supervised by an experimenter. During Session 3 we collected 298
more behavioural data, this time in person. Finally, in Session 4 we collected neuroimaging (MRI) data. 299
 300
Behavioural testing. Using established and published tests in cognitive psychology is crucial for addressing 301
the reproducibility crisis and curbing the proliferation of one-off tests, for ensuring that findings are accurate 302
and that they can be compared and replicated by other researchers
129
. Therefore, where possible, we chose 303
existing questionnaires and behavioural tasks, with the exception of an explicit morphosyntax learning test, 304
which we developed and piloted ourselves due to the lack of such a test in the field (see section "Pilot study"). 305
All instructions and tests were delivered in French. Where the French version of a test was not available, it 306
was adapted and checked by at least one first-language French user in the team. Questionnaire instructions 307
(Session 1) were presented in written form. Behavioural task instructions (Sessions 2 and 3) were read by a 308
commercial natural reader (https://www.naturalreaders.com/commercial/read), using the voice “Renee 309
France” (if not otherwise specified), at speed -1. fMRI task instructions were given by the experimenter via 310
a microphone. All technical alterations and task adaptations were related to 1) language of delivery, or 2) 311
adapting a paper & pencil test for computer-based delivery. All the above information is thoroughly 312
documented for each test in the Supplementary Information file, Table S1. An overview of the data collection 313
structure is given in Figure 3 and an extensive theoretical explanation of the tests included in sessions 1-3 314
can be found in our recent exploratory work on the behavioural correlates of language aptitude, which 315
included this sample

pilot-tested. ArtGram, designed for adults, extends the PLAB4 task used for language aptitude testing in 321

Technical Validation 539
In compliance with the BIDS indications for mixed raw and derivative datasets, to improve user experience 540
and reduce redundancy, we chose to include pre-processed behavioural data as derivative tables. We also 541
provide quality control (QC) reports for all imaging modalities, together with the code for reproducing them. 542
Below we specify the details of technical validation for each data type. 543
Questionnaires 544
Questionnaire source data were retrieved from Qualtrics XM© and preprocessed in Python. Each 545
questionnaire was cleaned with its own script and then merged with the others via another script. The overall 546
process aimed at data cleaning, as scoring was generally performed via the Qualtrics graphical user interface. 547
1) Log file data collection: for each questionnaire, from the source tabular file containing all participants, 548
we eliminated irrelevant metadata. In rare cases, when duplicates were identified we eliminated them at 549
source level by keeping the first non-empty instance of the questionnaire, blind to the contents, to avoid 550
selection bias. 551
2) Data cleaning and handling of missing data: in all questionnaires, string responses were recoded as 552
numbers. Missing data (NaN) was handled as follows: as a general rule, within-questionnaire, all 553
responses were forced (i.e. progression was halted if a response was missing or on-screen warnings 554
appeared to highlight unfilled responses). When information was indeed unavailable for a participant, 555

Behavioural data: correlations and reliability. Behavioural scores, whether resulting from tasks or 670
questionnaires (here we generally refer to them together as "behavioural data", and we use "scores" or 671
"measures" when referring specifically to their outcomes), derive from item-level data. To validate these 672
measures, we first explored them via correlations, and then ran reliability analyses in the form of internal 673
consistency coefficients, in a conceptually similar approach to our recent exploratory analysis of behavioural 674
data with a larger cohort
17
. Intercorrelation of the scores obtained from behavioural data (tasks and 675
questionnaires) was minimal, and reliability was acceptable to good in most cases (please refer to the 676
Supplementary Information file for com. The code for calculating Pearson's correlations on the z-scored data 677
as well as Cronbach Alpha, along with item-level reliability data and summary output (figures, tables) are 678
available in /code/validation/beh/ and in the Supplementary Information (Figures S2 and S3, 679
Tables S2, S3, S4, S5). 680
 681
To better understand the correlation patterns and reliability of these data, some considerations must 682
be made about correlations and about reliability measured by internal consistency. Regarding correlations, 683
on the one hand, high intercorrelations can be informative about shared variance between tests, but on the 684
other hand, having an excessively intercorrelated dataset can lead to problems linked to multicollinearity
149–
685
152
. On a conceptual level, the separability of latent variables facilitates the interpretation of the overall 686
construct. Our choices, when planning this data collection and selecting the tests, aimed at isolating the best 687
measures to represent each variable that we wanted to test in the context of this exploratory project on 688
language aptitude. All things considered, a relative degree of independence of our measures was not only 689
expected, but desirable. In the Supplementary Information (Table S2), we report all Pearson pairwise linear 690
correlations having a coefficient of at least \|r(100)\| > .5 and significant at p < .05. In 691
/code/validation/beh/ code is provided to generate Table S2 and a matrix (Supplementary Figure 692
S2) to visualise these data (correlate\_matrix.py). Keeping in mind that correlations are just a 693
preliminary way to explore data, most significant correlations were between metrics within the same test, 694
and between these and their cumulative score, when present. As concerns correlations across tasks (marked 695
with \* and \*\* for positive and negative relationships respectively, in Table S2), the most evident patterns 696
emerging are the correlations between reading measures and those typically related to or predictive of 697
reading skill or deficit (all sub-scores from the RAN, text reading, word and pseudoword reading, spelling, 698
spoonerisms, phoneme suppression and non-word repetition); between the Brocanto and CVLT long-term 699
recognition score; between AMMA (total and tonal scores) and the musical training score from the 700
MUSEBAQ (time, intensity and level of practice reached). No other measures were highly and significantly 701
correlated. 702
Regarding reliability, the main advantage of internal consistency measured via Cronbach alpha is 703
that, as long as covariance is inspected and there are minimal to no nonignorable nonresponses (MNAR 704
data, i.e. missing not at random), it can be applied quite flexibly, unlike other methods
153
. Minimal reliability 705
requirements are a matter of debate, as the  value can be affected by the number of items and improved by 706
the independence of the construct these tap into
154–156
. This being said, it is harder to obtain a reliable 707

Brain imaging 745
Anatomical imaging anonymisation, QC and brain extraction. Facial features in anatomical MRI scans 746
violate the principle of anonymity in open data. Therefore, T1-weighted MPRAGE scans were defaced with 747
PyDeface (https://pypi.org/project/pydeface/) and their quality was assessed in the Computational Anatomy 748
Toolbox for SPM (hereon, CAT12)
161
. Specifically, we used the CAT12 segmentation and sample 749
homogeneity toolboxes, providing easily interpretable quality measures at the participant and group level. 750
The weighted overall image quality (IQR) and the quartic mean Z-score are the two key indicators of image 751
quality. IQR combines noise and spatial resolution measurements before pre-processing, while the mean 752
quartic Z-score assesses the homogeneity of data after pre-processing, with deviations increasing variance 753
and reducing statistical power. The product of IQR and quartic mean Z-scores combines these quality 754
measures, with a low number indicating high quality. For each participant, we provide a PDF with the CAT12 755
report, as well as information on sample homogeneity, in /code/validation/anat/cat12/. The 756
CAT12 toolbox can be easily run via a graphical user interface (GUI) in Matlab and requires no custom code. 757
We provide the group distribution of the described measures in Figure 14. 758
